column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Terbium Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/rare-earth/lanthanide/terbium-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Terbium oxide layer removal
Near-IR wavelength for optimal Terbium absorption
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning
Fluence threshold for Terbium oxide removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning
Number of cleaning passes for complete oxide removal
Optimal beam overlap for complete coverage

Typical range for Nd:YAG laser cleaning of reflective metals; starting at 100W to balance ablation and heat input. Terbium's low thermal conductivity (~11 W/mK) requires conservative power to avoid melting (mp 1356°C). Source: Extrapolated from metal cleaning studies (e.g., Kumar et al., Opt. Lasers Eng. 2018); manufacturer specs like IPG Photonics YLR series limit max to prevent substrate damage.

Effective speeds for contamination removal on metals; higher for light contaminants (e.g., oxides), lower for heavy. Recommended 1500 mm/s for Terbium to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ) due to poor thermal dissipation. Source: Industry standards from Laser Clean Corp. manuals; case studies on rare earth alloys (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for industrial cleaning to achieve plasma-mediated ablation with minimal HAZ; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but costlier. 20 ns recommended for Terbium to limit thermal diffusion. Source: Laser physics research on metal ablation (e.g., Ancona et al., Appl. Surf. Sci. 2010); avoids fs due to Terbium's reflectivity at 1064 nm.

Typical for pulsed cleaning; higher rates speed up process but risk heat accumulation in low-conductivity materials like Terbium. 30 kHz balances efficiency and safety. Trade-off: >50 kHz may cause subsurface heating. Source: Academic literature on Nd:YAG cleaning (e.g., Lee et al., J. Phys. D 2015); adapted for rare earth metals.

Fluence below Terbium's ablation threshold (~4 J/cm² estimated for 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal without substrate damage. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for light industrial cleaning. Terbium's high reflectivity (~70% at IR) requires higher fluence than dielectrics. Source: Ablation threshold studies on similar metals (e.g., Rubenchik et al., Appl. Opt. 2017); conservative due to limited Terbium-specific data.

Beam diameter for precise cleaning; smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for Terbium surface preparation to ensure uniform fluence without over-focusing heat. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems; optimized for metal reflectivity in industrial applications.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete Terbium cleaning without excessive exposure. Multi-pass allows lower fluence per pass to protect soft substrate. Source: Industry practice from case studies on alloy cleaning (e.g., Proc. SPIE 2019); Terbium's malleability favors fewer passes.

Ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for Terbium to avoid gaps or over-ablation, balancing speed and efficacy. Higher (70-80%) for heavy contaminants. Source: Process optimization studies (e.g., Katayama et al., J. Laser Micro/Nanoeng. 2012); standard for scanned laser cleaning of metals.
